w:r.w:t: Full lesson plan — 4 lessons, 20 hours, Weeks 3–4

Linux is the operating system that powers the cloud. More than 90% of public cloud workloads run on Linux, all major cloud providers offer Linux-based compute instances as their primary offering, and every cloud administrator, DevOps engineer, and solutions architect must be comfortable with the Linux command line. This module transforms learners from cloud consumers to cloud operators — by the end of Week 4, every learner will have a running web server they configured entirely from the command line.

All CLI labs in this module require a live Linux environment. The recommended setup is an Ubuntu 22.04 LTS EC2 t2.micro instance (free tier eligible) per learner provisioned in the AWS af-south-1 (Cape Town) region. The following must be confirmed before Lesson 1 begins:
Each learner has an active AWS Free Tier account (set up in Module 1)
EC2 instance is running Ubuntu 22.04 LTS in af-south-1
Security group allows inbound SSH (port 22) from the class IP range and HTTP (port 80) from anywhere
Learners can connect via SSH using PuTTY (Windows) or terminal (Mac/Linux)
Fallback: browser terminal at https://bellard.org/jslinux is tested and working
A second shared EC2 instance is available for SSH/SCP exercises in Lesson 4
On completion of Module 2 learners will be able to:
Explain the history and philosophy of Linux and its role in cloud infrastructure
Navigate the Linux file system hierarchy using the command line
Create, move, copy, and delete files and directories using core shell commands
Interpret and modify file permissions and ownership using chmod, chown, and octal notation
Manage software packages using apt (Debian/Ubuntu) and describe yum/dnf for RHEL
Use text editors (nano and vi) to create and edit configuration files
Write and execute a basic Bash shell script to automate a repetitive task
Monitor system processes, memory, and disk using ps, top, df, and free
Connect to a remote Linux server via SSH and transfer files using SCP
The following commands are taught, practised, and assessed in this module. Learners receive this as a printed reference card to keep throughout the course.
Linux origins, philosophy & the file system
Explain why Linux dominates cloud and server infrastructure
Identify major Linux distributions and their use cases
Describe the Linux file system hierarchy (FHS) structure
Navigate the file system using pwd, ls, and cd
Explain the difference between relative and absolute paths
Lab environment: Ubuntu 22.04 LTS on AWS EC2 free tier (t2.micro) — pre-provisioned per learner
Terminal access: SSH via PuTTY (Windows) or native terminal (Mac/Linux)
Slides: Linux history timeline and FHS diagram
Handout: Linux distributions comparison card (one per learner)
Fallback: browser-based terminal at https://bellard.org/jslinux (test before class)

What runs the internet? (20 min) — Facilitator shows learners that Android (Linux kernel), most web servers (Apache/Nginx on Linux), AWS EC2 instances, and even the Johannesburg Stock Exchange run Linux. Ask: 'How many of you have used Linux today without knowing it?'

A brief history of Linux (30 min) — Unix origins (1969, Bell Labs) → GNU project (Stallman, 1983) → Linux kernel (Linus Torvalds, 1991, a 21-year-old student). Open source philosophy: free, no vendor lock-in — why it matters for African developers. The Linux Foundation today.

Linux distributions landscape (30 min) — Debian family: Ubuntu (most common for learners and cloud), Debian, Kali Linux. Red Hat family: RHEL, CentOS, Fedora. Cloud context: Amazon Linux 2023, Ubuntu on Azure. Which distro for what purpose. This course uses Ubuntu 22.04 LTS.

First terminal connection (20 min) — Facilitator live-demos SSH connection to EC2 instance. Shows the shell prompt anatomy: user@hostname:~$. Explains what each part means. Learners observe before attempting themselves.

Lab 2a — Connect and explore (30 min) — Each learner SSHs into their lab instance. Tasks: (1) confirm login with whoami, (2) run uname -a and record the kernel version, (3) run uptime, (4) take a screenshot. Facilitator circulates. Common issue: SSH key permissions — document the fix in the lab guide.

The Linux file system hierarchy (45 min) — Everything is a file in Linux. FHS standard: / (root), /home (user directories), /etc (config files), /var (logs, variable data), /tmp (temporary), /usr (user programs), /bin and /sbin (system binaries), /proc and /sys (virtual kernel interfaces), /dev (devices), /mnt and /media (mount points). Visual diagram shown and discussed.

Navigation commands (30 min) — pwd (where am I?), ls (what is here?), ls -la (full detail including permissions), cd (move around), cd .. (up one level), cd ~ (home directory), cd / (root). Absolute vs relative paths explained with a SA analogy: absolute = full street address including province; relative = 'turn left at the traffic light'.

Lab 2a continued — Navigation challenge (60 min) — Learners complete a guided worksheet: navigate to /etc and list contents; navigate to /var/log and list the 5 most recent files; navigate to /proc and read the cpuinfo file; return to home directory. Each step confirmed with a screenshot. Submitted as Lab 2a Part 2.
CLI cheat sheet build (20 min) — Learners collaboratively fill in the first section of their personal CLI cheat sheet (provided template). Facilitator reviews; previews Lesson 2 on file operations.
Lab 2a: SSH screenshot and navigation worksheet submitted to LMS
CLI cheat sheet section 1 completed and reviewed by facilitator
SSH issues are almost guaranteed in the first lab session — build a 20-minute buffer into setup or pre-provision instances before class
Browser fallback (jslinux) is essential if AWS access fails — test it personally before every session
Linus Torvalds was 21 and a student when he wrote Linux — this detail lands well with young SA learners
Emphasise: /etc is pronounced 'et-see', not 'forward-slash-et-cee' — correct this early to build professional vocabulary
File operations, text editing & the shell
Create, copy, move, and delete files and directories
View and search file contents using cat, less, grep, and pipe
Edit files using nano and understand basic vi/vim modes
Use wildcards and tab completion effectively
Same Ubuntu EC2 lab instance from Lesson 1
Lab worksheet: file operations challenge (printed and available in LMS)
Nano quick-reference card (provided — laminated if possible)
Vi/vim survival cheat sheet (1 page, provided)
Navigation speed drill (20 min) — Facilitator calls out directory names; learners race to navigate there and confirm with pwd. Top 3 fastest recognised. Reviews any outstanding Lab 2a issues.

File and directory operations (40 min) — touch (create empty file), mkdir / mkdir -p (create directory with parents), cp (copy), mv (move or rename), rm (remove), rm -rf (remove recursively — use with extreme caution). Wildcards: *, ?, [abc]. Tab completion demo. Common mistakes and recovery steps.

Lab 2b Part 1 — File system construction (40 min) — Learners build a directory structure from a specification: /home/ubuntu/gda-project/{logs,config,scripts,data}. Create 3 placeholder files in each directory. Copy the config directory to /tmp. Rename the logs directory to archive. Remove the data directory. Confirm final structure with ls -R. Screenshot submitted.

Viewing and searching file contents (35 min) — cat (print whole file), less (page through), head -n (first N lines), tail -n (last N lines), tail -f (follow live updates — great for log monitoring). grep 'pattern' file — basic search. grep -r (recursive search). Pipe ( | ): chain commands together. Example: grep 'error' /var/log/syslog | tail -20.

I/O redirection (25 min) — stdout (> overwrite, >> append), stderr (2>), stdin (<). Practical examples: echo 'Hello GDA' > hello.txt; cat /etc/passwd > users.txt; ls -la >> audit.log. Why this matters in cloud: writing logs, building config files, scripting automation.

Nano live walkthrough (20 min) — Open, write, save (Ctrl+O), exit (Ctrl+X), search (Ctrl+W). Facilitator creates /home/ubuntu/gda-project/config/app.conf with 5 key=value pairs live. Learners follow along on their own instances.

Vi/vim — survival skills (30 min) — Three modes: normal, insert (press i), command (press :). Open a file, enter insert mode, type text, return to normal mode, save (:w), quit (:q), force quit (:q!). The well-known joke about how to exit vim is used deliberately to normalise the learning curve.

Lab 2b Part 2 — Edit and search challenge (60 min) — Tasks: (1) use nano to create a welcome message file, (2) use grep to find all lines containing 'ubuntu' in /etc/passwd, (3) redirect the output of df -h to disk-report.txt, (4) use tail -f on /var/log/syslog for 60 seconds and capture 10 lines. Submit Lab 2b via LMS.
Pipes and redirection patterns (15 min) — Learners share the most useful one-liner discovered during lab. Facilitator adds 3 bonus power commands: sort, uniq, wc -l.
Lab 2b: file structure screenshot and search/edit tasks submitted to LMS
Facilitator checks vi/vim exit — any learner still stuck gets 1:1 support
rm -rf deserves a dedicated safety warning — consider a live demo of an accidental deletion and the lesson it teaches
Tab completion transforms the learning experience — enforce it as a habit from this lesson onwards
Vim panic is common and can be made humorous — lean into it, the lesson becomes memorable
The pipe concept is often a lightbulb moment for learners — give it extra time and use multiple real examples
Read and interpret the Linux permissions string (rwxr-xr--)
Modify permissions using chmod with symbolic and octal notation
Change file ownership with chown and group with chgrp
Create users and groups and manage sudo access
Install, update, and remove packages using apt
Explain the difference between apt and yum/dnf for RHEL-based systems
Handout: permissions reference card (rwx grid and octal table, printed)
Lab worksheet: user and permissions scenario (printed)
Slides: chmod octal notation visual (3-bit binary explanation)
Port 80 must be open in the EC2 security group before class — pre-configure this

Why permissions matter (20 min) — Real breach scenario: a misconfigured S3 bucket (world-readable) exposed 500,000 SA patient records. The root cause is traced back to the Linux permission model. Sets the security stakes for the entire lesson.

Reading the permissions string (45 min) — Anatomy of ls -la output. File type indicator (-/d/l). Three triads: owner, group, other. r=4, w=2, x=1. Worked examples: rwxr-xr-- = 754. Common patterns: 755 (executable script), 644 (config file), 600 (private SSH key), 777 (dangerous — never use on a server). Sticky bit and setuid briefly introduced.

chmod, chown, and chgrp (30 min) — chmod u+x script.sh (symbolic), chmod 644 file.txt (octal). chown ubuntu:devteam file.txt. chgrp developers /var/app. Sudo: running commands as root. The /etc/sudoers file and the sudo group. NEVER use chmod 777 on production systems.

Lab 2c Part 1 — Permissions clinic (40 min) — Scenario: learners are the sysadmin for a fictional SA fintech startup. Tasks: create a file with sensitive data (permissions 600); create a shared team directory writable only by the app group (770); create a public-facing script (755); intentionally break permissions on a test file then fix them. Submit screenshot of ls -la output.

Users and groups (35 min) — adduser username, userdel -r username, passwd username, usermod -aG groupname user. /etc/passwd and /etc/shadow explained — never store plain text passwords. id, whoami, and groups commands. Creating a service account with no login shell for an application — a common cloud and DevOps practice.

Package management with apt (35 min) — apt update (refresh package index), apt upgrade (apply available updates), apt install package, apt remove, apt purge, apt search term, apt show package. The /etc/apt/sources.list file. Why keeping systems updated is a critical security practice. Brief comparison with yum/dnf for Red Hat-based distributions.

Lab 2c Part 2 — User management and package install (60 min) — Tasks: (1) create a new user named webadmin and add to the sudo group, (2) set a password and test sudo access, (3) install the nginx web server via apt, (4) verify nginx is running with systemctl status nginx, (5) access the default nginx page via the EC2 public IP in a browser. Submit Lab 2c — this is the first running service deployment of the course.

Cloud security connection (20 min) — Facilitator connects lesson concepts to Module 5 preview: least privilege principle, service accounts, and patch management. 'Everything you just did is what a cloud sysadmin does every day.'
Lab 2c: permissions screenshots and nginx running confirmation submitted to LMS
User creation verified: facilitator SSHs as webadmin to confirm access works correctly
The data breach story sets a serious and motivating tone — use it, it makes the permissions lesson memorable
Octal notation is confusing at first — use the 3-bit binary analogy: r=100(binary)=4, w=010=2, x=001=1
Lab 2c Part 2 ending with nginx visible in a browser is a major motivational milestone — celebrate it with the class
Port 80 must be pre-opened in the EC2 security group before class — if this is missed, the lab cannot complete
Write a Bash script with variables, conditionals, and loops
Make scripts executable and schedule them with cron
Monitor CPU, memory, disk, and processes in real time
Connect to remote servers using SSH key pairs (passwordless)
Transfer files between servers using SCP and rsync
Pass the Module 2 formative quiz at 70% or above
A second EC2 instance or shared class instance for SSH/SCP exercises
Bash scripting reference card (one per learner, provided)

Introduction to Bash scripting (40 min) — What is a shell script and why automate? The shebang line (#!/bin/bash). Variables: NAME='Ubuntu'. echo and read (user input). Conditionals: if/then/else/fi. Loops: for and while. Exit codes: $? where 0 = success. Practical cloud use cases: startup scripts, backup jobs, automated health checks.

Write a health-check script live (30 min) — Facilitator writes from scratch: a script that checks disk usage (df -h), memory (free -m), and running process count (ps aux | wc -l), then prints a summary report. Learners follow along on their own instances. chmod +x and run it together.

Lab 2d Part 1 — Write your own automation script (50 min) — Learners write a backup script that: (1) creates a timestamped directory in /tmp/backups/, (2) copies /home/ubuntu/gda-project into it, (3) prints 'Backup complete: [timestamp]' to a log file. Must complete with exit code 0. Peer review: swap scripts with a classmate and run each other's. Submit Lab 2d-1.

Cron jobs — scheduling automation (20 min) — crontab -e to edit. Cron syntax: minute hour day month weekday. Examples: daily backup at 2am (0 2 * * *), hourly log rotation (0 * * * *). Why cron matters in cloud operations: certificate renewal, database backups, scheduled health checks.

System monitoring commands (30 min) — top (real-time CPU and memory), htop (improved version — install via apt), ps aux (all running processes), kill / killall (terminate a process by PID or name), df -h (disk space), du -sh * (directory sizes), free -m (RAM in megabytes), uptime and load average interpretation. Scenario: diagnosing a high-load server.

SSH key pairs and secure remote access (30 min) — Public and private key cryptography in plain language using the padlock analogy. ssh-keygen to generate a key pair. The ~/.ssh/authorized_keys file. SSH config file (~/.ssh/config) for server aliases. Disabling password authentication as a security best practice. SSH tunnels briefly mentioned for context.

Lab 2d Part 2 — SSH key setup and SCP file transfer (30 min) — Tasks: (1) generate an SSH key pair on the lab instance, (2) copy the public key to the second shared instance using ssh-copy-id, (3) SSH into the second instance without a password, (4) SCP the backup script to the second instance, (5) verify the script runs on the remote server. Submit Lab 2d-2.

Module 2 Formative Quiz (30 min) — 20 multiple-choice questions covering all four lessons. Distribution: Linux history and distributions (3), file system and navigation (4), file operations and text editing (4), permissions and users (5), scripting and monitoring (4). Minimum pass: 14 out of 20 (70%).

Module review and Module 3 preview (25 min) — Review quiz answers and re-teach any question missed by more than 40% of the class. Assign pre-reading: 'What is an IP address?' one-page handout. Preview: 'You can now control a Linux server from the command line. Next, you will connect it to a network and the internet.'
Lab 2d-1: backup script submitted and peer-reviewed — facilitator checks exit codes
Lab 2d-2: SSH passwordless login and SCP file transfer confirmed
Module 2 Quiz: 20 questions, 70% pass mark, results recorded in LMS
Learners below 70% offered a 1:1 catch-up session before Module 3 begins
The live scripting demo is the most impactful moment of the module — type slowly, explain every line, and expect questions
Peer script review is intentional: reading someone else's code is a foundational professional skill
SSH key setup is a hard prerequisite for Module 4 cloud labs — ensure every learner succeeds before leaving this session
If time is short, trim the cron section to a conceptual overview and protect lab time for SSH keys and SCP
4.6 Module 2 Quiz — Full Question Bank (20 Questions)

The following 20 questions constitute the Module 2 formative quiz. Administer on paper or via the LMS. Minimum pass mark: 14/20 (70%). Questions progress from foundational recall to applied problem-solving.
Q1. Which company maintains the Linux kernel today?
(Linux history — The Linux Foundation stewards the kernel)
Q2. What does the shebang line #!/bin/bash tell the operating system?
A) That the file is read-only
B) That bash should be used to interpret the script
C) That the file is encrypted
Q3. Which command shows your current directory?
Q4. What does ls -la display that ls does not?
B) Hidden files, permissions, ownership, and sizes
Q5. Which directory in the Linux FHS stores system-wide configuration files?
Q6. What is the correct command to create the directory structure /app/logs/archive in a single command?
Q7. A learner runs rm -rf /home/ubuntu/project by mistake. What is the most likely outcome?
A) The command will ask for confirmation before deleting
B) Only empty directories are removed
C) The entire project directory and all its contents are permanently deleted
D) Files are moved to the recycle bin
Q8. What does the pipe character ( | ) do in a Linux command?
A) Saves output to a file
B) Sends the output of one command as input to another
D) Appends output to an existing file
Q9. Which command lets you follow a log file in real time as new entries are written?
Q10. What does the permissions string rwxr-x--- mean?
A) Owner: read/write/execute; Group: read/execute; Others: no access
B) Owner: read only; Group: execute; Others: read/write
D) Owner: read/write; Group: read; Others: execute
Q11. A private SSH key should have which permissions to be accepted by SSH?
Q12. What is the octal value for permissions rw-r--r-- (a typical config file)?
Q13. Which command installs a software package on an Ubuntu server?
A) Switches to a different user permanently
B) Runs a command with root (superuser) privileges
C) Shows which user is logged in
D) Lists available users on the system
Q15. Which file contains the list of all user accounts on a Linux system?
Q16. What is the first line of a valid Bash script?
Q17. What exit code indicates a command completed successfully in Linux?
Q18. Which command shows real-time CPU and memory usage for all running processes?
Q19. What is the purpose of the ~/.ssh/authorized_keys file?
A) It stores the server's private key
B) It lists public keys that are allowed to log in without a password
C) It contains the SSH server configuration
D) It logs all SSH connection attempts
Q20. A learner wants to copy a script named backup.sh from their local server to a remote server at IP 10.0.0.5 into the /home/ubuntu/ directory. Which command is correct?
